construct_description: The ability to evaluate competing clinical and operational perspectives, engage constructively with team members, and drive toward a sound protocol recommendation — a core competency for paramedics and EMS supervisors involved in quality improvement and system-level decisions.

collaboration_construct.name: Collaboration Quality
collaboration_construct.description: How effectively the participant engages with teammates — building on ideas, challenging constructively, and synthesizing toward consensus.

dimensions.name: Engagement
dimensions.description: Actively participates and responds to teammates

dimensions.name: Building
dimensions.description: References, quotes, and extends others' ideas

dimensions.name: Challenging
dimensions.description: Pushes back constructively with clinical reasoning

dimensions.name: Synthesizing
dimensions.description: Combines viewpoints and moves toward a decision

domain_dimensions.name: Clinical Reasoning
domain_dimensions.description: Applies clinical evidence and protocol knowledge to the decision

domain_dimensions.name: Risk Assessment
domain_dimensions.description: Identifies operational, patient safety, and liability risks in the options

domain_dimensions.name: Evidence Use
domain_dimensions.description: References outcome data, protocols, or guidelines in arguments

domain_dimensions.name: Decision Quality
domain_dimensions.description: Final recommendation is well-justified, clinically sound, and operational

scenario.title: The Field Sedation Decision
scenario.prompt: Your EMS quality team must decide whether to recommend that the medical director formally add ketamine to the paramedic formulary for pre-hospital sedation. The agency has been operating under informal 'permission' for select paramedics — but three recent incidents have made it clear the agency needs a formal, consistent protocol. You need a recommendation for the medical director by end of shift.

teammates.name: Chen
teammates.title: Field Supervisor / Senior Paramedic
teammates.trait: Clinically confident and protocol-focused — skeptical of anything that adds liability without clear patient benefit, but respects strong evidence

teammates.name: Torres
teammates.title: EMS Educator / Training Officer
teammates.trait: Focused on training feasibility and crew competency — wants to say yes but needs to know the training infrastructure can support it

teammates.name: Reeves
teammates.title: EMS Medical Director's Liaison
teammates.trait: Pragmatic and policy-minded — needs a recommendation the medical director can actually sign off on and defend

decision_options.name: Full Adoption — All Paramedics, All Indications
decision_options.desc: Ketamine added to formulary for pain management, sedation, and behavioral emergencies. All certified paramedics. Standing orders for pain; online medical control for behavioral indications.

decision_options.name: Limited Adoption — Trauma/Pain Only, No Behavioral
decision_options.desc: Ketamine approved for pain management and post-intubation sedation only. Behavioral emergency indication excluded pending further evidence review.

decision_options.name: Pilot Program — Selected Paramedics, 90 Days
decision_options.desc: Approve 8–10 senior paramedics for a 90-day pilot with mandatory QI tracking. Expand based on outcomes.

decision_options.name: Defer — Complete Training Infrastructure First
decision_options.desc: No formal adoption until a full training module and competency evaluation is completed. Estimated timeline: 60–90 days.

scenario.reference_data_html:
Ketamine Pre-Hospital Evidence Summary

IndicationEvidence LevelNAEMSP PositionNotable Risk
Trauma pain managementStrong (meta-analysis, RCTs)EndorsedLow at correct dosing
Post-intubation sedationModerateEndorsedEmergence reactions (8%)
Behavioral emergenciesLimited / MixedConditional (online MD control)Elevated AE rate, litigation history
Procedural sedationModerateEndorsedLaryngospasm (2%)

Neighboring Agency Outcomes (12 months)

AgencyUsesAdverse EventsMedical Control Activations
Metro Fire EMS2146 (2.8%)41 (behavioral indication)
Valley Ambulance882 (2.3%)18

Training Requirements (Based on Regional Precedent)

  • Initial training: 4–6 hours (didactic + simulation)
  • Annual recertification: ~2 hours
  • Instructor qualification: requires paramedic + ketamine clinical experience
  • All neighboring agencies: 'manageable' training burden

Recent Agency Incidents (Catalyst for this Discussion)

  • Incident 1: Paramedic used ketamine for excited delirium — appropriate outcome, but no documentation of medical control contact
  • Incident 2: Ketamine administered for pain — dosing was correct but there was no standing order authorizing it
  • Incident 3: Paramedic declined to use ketamine for significant trauma pain — patient arrived in severe distress. Paramedic cited 'no protocol' as reason for withholding.

agenda.label: Round 1
agenda.title: Assess the Incidents
agenda.desc: Understand what the three incidents reveal about the current gap.

agenda.label: Round 2
agenda.title: Debate the Options
agenda.desc: Surface the tradeoffs. What are we actually deciding and who bears the risk?

agenda.label: Round 3
agenda.title: Land the Recommendation
agenda.desc: Converge on what the medical director can sign.

message_schedule.text: The three incidents say the same thing: we have paramedics using ketamine without a protocol, paramedics not using it when they should, and no consistent documentation when it does happen. That's a liability problem regardless of where we land on the clinical question. We can't keep operating in this gray zone.

I want to be honest about where our crew stands on training. We have strong paramedics who've been using ketamine informally — they're already competent. But we also have newer paramedics who've never touched it. Any protocol we adopt has to account for that gap, or we're creating a different kind of inconsistency problem.

From a medical director standpoint, the ask is: give me something I can sign and defend. The NAEMSP position gives us cover on pain management and post-intubation sedation — that's a strong endorsement. The behavioral emergency indication is where I'm cautious. There's litigation history nationally on that one and our medical director is going to ask about it specifically.

I want to push on the pilot option. We're not a research agency — a 90-day pilot with 8 paramedics doesn't give us enough data to change anything meaningful, and in the meantime we still have the same protocol gap for the other 40 paramedics. The pilot feels like a way to delay the decision rather than make it.

Chen's right that a full pilot is probably overkill — but I don't think we can do 'full adoption, all paramedics' without the training module. Incident 3 tells me some of our crew genuinely doesn't know when to use this. A protocol without training just means the wrong people use it confidently and the right people still hesitate.

Here's what I keep coming back to: the limited adoption option — pain and post-intubation only, no behavioral — is actually defensible with strong evidence and keeps us out of the contested territory. We can always add behavioral later once we have our own QI data. Does anyone have a clinical argument for why we *need* behavioral in the first protocol?

I'm landing on limited adoption with a mandatory training requirement before any paramedic uses it independently. Not a 90-day pilot — full adoption, but conditioned on completing the module. That solves the 'paramedics hesitating because no protocol' problem immediately for those who are trained, and gives Torres the training runway for everyone else.

That works for me if we're specific about what 'training complete' means — written competency, simulation pass, not just hours logged. And we need someone accountable for tracking who's cleared. I can build the module in 3 weeks if we decide today.

That's what I'd take to the medical director: limited adoption (pain + post-intubation sedation), standing orders for trained paramedics, online medical control for any behavioral emergency use until we revisit, and a 4–6 hour competency module as a prerequisite. That's a recommendation he can sign. What's the group's final position?

coaching_tips: Reference what teammates said by name — 'Chen's point about the liability gap' is stronger than a general restatement.
Push back with evidence, not just opinion. When you disagree, cite the NAEMSP position or neighboring agency data.
The hardest move in collaborative tasks is narrowing toward a decision. Move from debate to synthesis in Round 3.
Conditional adoption is often the most defensible answer — it acknowledges both the evidence and the risk.
Behavioral emergency indications are the contested territory here. Engaging that tension specifically shows clinical depth.
